不过疫情也不是完全没有好处,尤其对于我们可以 work from home 的专业。因为疫情各国政府都印了很多钱,英国也不例外,除了给人民发钱,给企业撒钱,总是会剩下一点给科研。2020年11月中心拿到了ESRC Quick Spending的项目 (美其名曰 World Class Research Infrastructure: £213m to upgrade the UK’s world-class research infrastructure and Urban Big Data Centre receives further award from UKRI-ESRC),2021 年三月底就要把钱花完。于是从11月开始就和同事一起各种忙着打广告和招人。因为之前proposal是需要做 travel survey,但是covid做不了,就临时修改了 proposal 的内容,买了格拉斯哥大地区的mobile phone app数据,最后整个项目变成做了2021 年一整年的mobile phone app研究。从结果上来说是很好的,一是这是一个非常热门而且有实际意义的方向。中心和格拉斯哥市政府在过去的五六年有长期的合作,这次他们听说我们买了这个数据,就连带给我们钱帮他们做了半年多的政府项目。项目内容主要是利用手机 app 数据来理解人们在疫情期间的出行模式,比如 home location estimation,create OD matrix,和计算一些 place visitation pattern。第二是数据方面,因为和数据服务商(Huq Data and Tamoco Data)的合作都还算愉快,各方的项目需求也很多,于是中心打算继续购买他们过去 3 年和未来 2 年全英国的数据,之后有条件的分享给大家做非营利性的学术研究。第三是在非常短的时间内雇佣了 1 位非常优秀的博后,同时延长了另一位博后的合同,为接下来的研究奠定了基础。用新型数据做科研真是个蓝海,能做的东西不少。以前学术界没有数据或者买不起得不到高质量数据,公司没有时间,资源,和知识来做一些对社会有用但是不赚钱的数据分析,现在通过政府科研项目资金购买公司数据,分发给学术界做研究,既节约了大量的一对一的合同谈判时间,也简化了流程,提高了效率。

关于中心在 mobility 研究方面的新闻:How people move around cities matters

除了在中心做关于新型数据的研究,我和同事还在做一个关注于室内环境质量的项目(Building capacity for sensor-enhanced surveys)。虽然是一个小项目,但是因为项目里面不仅仅涉及到社会调查的部分,还会涉及到到很多硬件和软件相关的传感器集成,总体来说还是挺好玩的。除此之外,我个人还是一直对高分辨率热遥感,三维城市,空间区位优化,还有机器学习的应用非常感兴趣。夏天的时候和 ASU 的好友一起组织了一期Google Summer of Code,巴西小哥帮我们把一些基本的空间区位优化的算法写到了pysal里面,文章还在joss审稿。希望明年夏天能一起再做一期,进一步提升空间优化方法的可重复性和开放性。A summer of python coding - open-source facility location modelling (Spopt) development

今年加入了springer的urban book series的editorial board (https://www.springer.com/series/14773/editors)。一个好处就是可以拿到这个系列里之后所有新书的free copy。感觉城市科学方面的研究实在太广了,我到现在还没收到一本能好好看的下去的书,还有可能就是行业内还是更认学术期刊,总觉得 book chapter 少了点严谨。今年下半年加入了英国地理学家协会 (RGS-IBG) 的Quantitative Methods Research Group (Committee),还是希望能继续推动定量方法在地理学和城市科学里的应用。因为之前在 AAG 的 SAM 服务过 3 年,希望明年能推动一些英美之间的合作研究。

今年年底之前中心中了接下来2年半的延长项目(Urban Big Data Centre’s data service awarded new funding from ESRC),总共四百万英镑。从2022 年1月份开始中心有一系列的招聘,包括两名数据科学家 (Data Scientist (Digital Storytelling Engineer) at University of Glasgow and Data Scientist at University of Glasgow),一名城市遥感和传感方面的博后(广告一月份会发出来,主要由我负责,欢迎大家垂询),还有一名兼职的项目管理人员(Project Officer at University of Glasgow),如果有合适的欢迎大家申请和咨询。除此之外,明年目前确定的是在三月份左右会招一名新的博士生,是和英国的 Charity Salvation Army 合作一起分析他们的 employment+ program。除了这些已经确定的招聘机会以外,还有四五个proposal在等待最终的结果,如果有额外的博士或者博士后名额,会继续发广告招聘。

最后谈谈今年的文章。今年总共发表了 4 篇 journal articles,1 篇 book chapter,1 篇GIScience 的会议论文。其中 book chapter 和一篇期刊是出自第一年MSc学生的项目,会议是今年手机app数据研究的一个简单汇总,还有另外2篇期刊是关于优化和城市热岛的合作文章。唯一的一篇一作是把 2018 年博后期间做的研究收尾了,也算是有始有终。引用方面没有什么特别的突破,还是老样子不温不火。一直以来对于科研的追求还是在于长期的科研兴趣(有趣但是往往没有什么直接的用处)和实际的社会价值(有用,尤其在现在的社科领域),谁知道哪天热点就落自己头上了呢。当然这些都是基于英国从助理教授就开始的长期合同制度带来的某种程度上的学术自由吧,如果要评 tenured 自然是有另外一种玩法。现在在审的文章有个 3-4 篇,加上和去年的 MSc 同学一起做的 2 篇深度学习应用的文章,和中心博后一起做的mobile phone app 和 housing sensor enhance survey 的 3-4 篇文章,还有一些顺理成章要写的 proposal,明年应该还是会有很多写作任务。

新年愿望,就希望疫情能消散,让我能在 2022 年开次线下的会议,再去一次欧陆吧。感谢所有同事朋友老师合作者的帮助和支持,还有家人一直以来的支持和陪伴。在我来英国以后,过去的 2 年半里我有三位亲人离开了人世,19 年还有一位从小非常要好的同学也不在了,我自己也有 6 年时间没有回中国。所以唯一的愿望就是希望大家都能身体健康,平安快乐,期待下一次的相会。
entry.summary: Teaching through lockdowns, mobile phone app data as a research blue ocean, the first Google Summer of Code, and hopes for reunions.

entry.title: A summer of Python coding — open-source facility location modelling (spopt) development

link.title: A summer of Python coding — open-source facility location modelling (spopt) development

entry.content:
Originally published on the Urban Big Data Centre blog on 16 September 2021 (by Germano Barcelos, James Gaboardi, Levi Wolf and Qunshan Zhao). The original page is no longer online — an archived copy is available on the Wayback Machine, including the figures omitted here.

Since its launch in 2005, the Google Summer of Code programme has introduced over 16,000 students to open-source software development.

This global initiative has partnered participants with 13,000 mentors from over 118 countries worldwide to work on a ten-week programming project. In 2021, Germano Barcelos worked with three mentors from PySAL and NumFOCUS to develop a facility location modelling module to optimally solve facility location problems under various distance metrics.

In this blog, the project team explain the four facility location models developed by Germano and share the future plans for this project.

Background to the project

Facility Location Modelling first appeared more than 100 years ago during the industrial revolution. Businesses needed to find the optimal locations to place factories to reduce logistical costs or build retail centres to generate more revenue from customers. Since then, researchers have developed coverage models to answer questions such as where to locate fire stations to fully cover the residential neighbourhoods within a given distance/time threshold or build a defined number of wireless towers to cover a certain percentage of the wireless communication network. Most facility location modelling problems have been formulated as mixed-integer programming problems. Given these problems are computationally difficult (NP-hard), they require the most powerful solvers from availing in Operations Research to find a solution in a tolerable period of time. Facility location modelling is also a geographical problem, so we need to work with spatial datasets such as road networks and polygon areas and use GIS to facilitate the data preparation, processing, storage, and visualisation.

In one recent review paper, Chen et al. (2021) discussed open source and proprietary software developed for facility location modelling (Location Set Covering Problem (LSCP) and Maximal Coverage Location Problem (MCLP)) including Microsoft Excel (FLP Solver), ArcGIS, R (Maxcovr), and Python (PySpatialOpt). The first two solutions are rooted in commercial software, using a heuristic approach to solve the MCLP and LSCP. Alternatively, Maxcovr and PySpatialOpt are based on open-source software (R/Python) and use an exact approach to solve the same models with generalised optimisation solvers. However, all these tools only provide very limited options for various metrics in the facility location modelling such as facility capacity, distance metrics, demand object shapes, demand weights, and solution approach. None of the software here can provide a single ‘one-stop’ open-source ecosystem to comprehensively address facility location modelling problems for the general users.

During the Google Summer of Code 2021, Germano developed four facility location models including LSCP/MCLP/P-Center/P-Median models in the PySAL Spopt repository. More information about these models can be found in books by Church, R. L., & Murray, A. T.: Business Site Selection, Location Analysis and GIS and Location covering models: History, applications and advancements. All models are solved by using an exact approach with PULP and can compute distance matrices with different metrics, such as Euclidean and Manhattan. The module provides the objective values for each model. Furthermore, for the P-Median model, the module can compute the mean distance of the objective value and the MCLP module can compute the percentage of model coverage.

Model evaluation and validation

For validation purposes, we first tested the MCLP results with the same example in Chen et al. (2021). It used U.S. census tract centroids as demand points and 16 candidate store sites. The objective is to cover the maximum amount of demand points given the population in the 2000s for each demand point given that only 4 stores can be located within the area. The MCLP results are shown in Figure 1. The figure shows where stores are sited to cover the maximum population in census tracts. The percentage of demand points covered is 87.76% and 21 points are not covered by any of the stores (white dots in the figure).

Figure 1. MCLP results from Pysal Spopt package (Jupyter Notebook example).

Moreover, we used the same dataset to solve the other three problems supported by the new module developed. Figure 2 represents the solution for the LSCP problem. The objective of this model is to minimize the number of facilities placed to cover all the demand points. Figure 2 shows that 8 facility sites must be placed to achieve full coverage.

Figure 2. LSCP results from Pysal Spopt package (Jupyter Notebook example).

Figure 3 displays the P-Median problem example. This model aims to minimize the sum of the travel distances from demand to supply by locating a fixed number of facilities. We located 4 facilities in our example here. Besides that, the population in each centroid is the weight of the model. In the solution results, the mean distance is approximately 2982.13 meters.

Figure 3. P-median results from Pysal Spopt package (Jupyter Notebook example).

Figure 4 displays the P-Center model that aims to minimize the maximum distance between a demand point and store site with a fixed number of stores to be placed. We located 4 facilities in our example here. The maximum distance of a demand point to a facility site in this solution is 7403.06 meters.

Figure 4. P-Center results from Pysal Spopt package (Jupyter Notebook example).

Future plans

We plan in the future to add capacity constraints in models and aim to provide a polygon partial coverage, which is another feature that no library supports yet. Since this is an open-source project, feel free to contribute your code and raise issues at our GitHub repository.

Note: One thing to remember is the solver wrapper PULP used by our module as well as PySpatialOpt. PULP makes it possible to use many solvers like Gurobi, CPLEX, CBC, and other solvers if they are installed. Therefore, the module can be used to solve problems that require a large amount of resources.

Final thoughts on the project from Germano

“Before the start of the programme, I did not have any background on the facility location modelling topic and this inspired me to work hard to get a good understanding of it to develop the module. During the summer, working on an open-source project introduced me to a new world of developing tests and running them automatically using continuous integration and delivery (CI/CD) tools like GitHub Actions. Besides that, I managed to know how the open-source community works were marvellous. Furthermore, I have significantly improved my communication skills including how to elaborate what I was thinking objectively and discuss ideas with my mentors to solve the problems in the best way.”

Project Team

  • Germano Barcelos, Universidade Federal de Viçosa, Brazil

  • Dr James Gaboardi, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, USA

  • Dr Levi John Wolf, School of Geographical Sciences, University of Bristol, UK

  • Dr Qunshan Zhao, UBDC and Urban Studies, University of Glasgow, UK
entry.summary: Since its launch in 2005, the Google Summer of Code programme has introduced over 16,000 students to open-source software development.
